tweets.text:
On this day in 1215, King John sealed the Magna Carta at Runnymede. Most people remember it as a document about liberty and justice. It was really about money. Over 50 of its 63 clauses addressed specific financial grievances: illegal taxes, seized property, rigged courts that enriched the crown. The barons who forced John's hand weren't philosophers. They were landlords tired of getting shaken down.

The document established one principle that would outlast everything else in it: the king could not levy taxes without consent of the governed. That idea shaped the English Parliament, the American Revolution, and every tax debate since. When colonists dumped tea into Boston Harbor 558 years later, they were making the same argument the barons made at Runnymede.

It reads today like a founding text of property rights. At the time, it was a debt negotiation backed by the threat of civil war.

tweets.text:
🇫🇷 Quelle immense fierté de voir le Master in Finance de l’ESCP Business School être classé #1 mondial par le Financial Times pour la quatrième année consécutive.

C’est une reconnaissance exceptionnelle, mais au-delà du classement, c’est surtout la consécration d’une aventure collective, portée par des femmes et des hommes profondément engagés : les étudiants, les alumni, les professeurs, les équipes pédagogiques, les équipes carrières et l’ensemble des partenaires qui font vivre ce programme au quotidien.

Ce résultat dit beaucoup de choses. Il dit la qualité académique du Master. Il dit la force d’un réseau international unique. Il dit l’exigence, l’ambition et l’énergie des étudiants. Il dit aussi la capacité de l’ESCP à former des profils capables de réussir dans un univers financier toujours plus compétitif, plus international et plus exigeant.

Être #1 mondial est déjà remarquable. Le rester quatre années de suite est le signe d’une vraie profondeur, d’une constance, et d’une culture de l’excellence qui ne se décrète pas mais qui se construit année après année.

Je suis particulièrement fier de cette réussite, fier d'avoir contribué à cette dynamique, et fier de voir l’ESCP continuer à rayonner au plus haut niveau mondial.

Un immense bravo à Philippe Thomas, à toutes les équipes du Master in Finance, aux étudiants, aux alumni et à tous ceux qui rendent cette réussite possible.

C’est une très belle page pour l’ESCP, et surtout une magnifique reconnaissance pour toute une communauté. 💙

*Lien (FT) : https://t.co/mEkfHu5idD

#ESCP #MasterInFinance #FinancialTimes #Finance

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #Housing | #China Cities Step Up Property Aid to Cut Buying Costs ⚠

➡ Chinese municipalities are intensifying property support measures by expanding housing subsidy programs and optimizing provident fund rules, moves aimed at lowering home-buying costs and stabilizing a sluggish real estate market, according to a report by the Securities Daily.

➡ Local governments across China have issued more than 470 property-related optimization policies so far this year, according to data from China Index Academy. 

➡ Of those, over 250 measures specifically targeted housing provident funds, focusing on raising loan ceilings, expanding withdrawal use cases, and supporting people with odd jobs or non-local workers.

➡ The continuous roll-out of local measures is successfully altering market sentiment despite short-term headwinds like summer heat and seasonal lulls. Yan Yuejin, vice president of the Shanghai E-House Real Estate Research Institute, told the paper that market expectations and confidence regarding price trends have “significantly strengthened,” lifting overall transaction activity to a new baseline.

➡ An official at the China Index Academy predicted that more cities will fast-track variations of these housing fund adjustments to help anchor the broader property market.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/es8IbFFBVw

tweets.text:
🇬🇧 In London, job openings for some white-collar roles have collapsed. Even if AI does not explain everything, the old model of knowledge work is clearly starting to break.

➡️ Some roles that used to sit at the heart of London’s service economy are now down to double-digit openings but we need to be careful as 2022 was a very specific period, with a still-tight labor market, post-Covid overhiring, tech euphoria and much more favorable financial conditions. Since then, rates have risen, the UK economy has slowed, banks and consulting firms have been looking to cut costs, and M&A/advisory activity has normalized.

🤔 In my view, AI is compressing the pyramid. Demand for senior profiles remains strong but junior generalists and repetitive task-based roles are much more exposed. The problem is that these tasks were also training tasks, so if companies cut junior cohorts too aggressively, they may improve margins in the short term but weaken their talent pipeline in the medium term.

The people who will come out ahead will not simply be those who use AI but those who know how to use it to become better at a real profession.

*Bloomberg link: https://t.co/VQU4kKAbsL

Since then, rates have risen, the UK economy has slowed, banks and consulting firms have been looking to cut costs, and M&A/advisory activity has normalized.

🤔 In my view, AI is compressing the pyramid. Demand for senior profiles remains strong but junior generalists and repetitive task-based roles are much more exposed. The problem is that these tasks were also training tasks, so if companies cut junior cohorts too aggressively, they may improve margins in the short term but weaken their talent pipeline in the medium term.

The people who will come out ahead will not simply be those who use AI but those who know how to use it to become better at a real profession.

*Bloomberg link: https://t.co/VQU4kKAbsL

tweets.text:
🇯🇵 The market is massively rebuilding one of the most powerful, but also one of the riskiest trades out there: shorting the yen 🚨

📉 Many investors are once again betting on further yen weakness, even as the currency trades near a very sensitive level around 160 against the dollar, and have built their largest bearish positions on the yen since 2017

➡️ Their logic is that as long as Japanese rates remain far below US rates, the yen carry trade remains attractive. Investors borrow in yen at a low cost, then reinvest that money into higher-yielding assets or currencies. As long as volatility stays low, this trade keeps paying, which is why the yen remains under pressure despite the Bank of Japan’s gradual rate hikes and despite the risk of FX intervention from Japanese authorities.

⚠️ Market now seems to see these risks as largely priced in. A BoJ rate hike is no longer a real surprise, neither is intervention from the Ministry of Finance. Many investors even see yen rebounds triggered by intervention as opportunities to sell the currency again. However, the more extreme the short yen positioning becomes, the more fragile the trade gets. If the BoJ turns more aggressive than expected, if US yields fall sharply, or if a risk-off episode forces funds to reduce leverage, the yen can rebound violently because too many investors are positioned in the same direction.

The yen can still weaken gradually from here but the risk of a brutal squeeze rises as bearish positioning keeps building.

*Bloomberg link: https://t.co/m7tuV1Fb0C
